sentences.text: Well, [BACKROUND NOISE] we were really afraid.
We had heard that instead of water, gas comes on, and then the floors cave in.
But it didn't.
Water came, and it was, in fact, warm water.
And we showered.
And--
Yes, with my mother.
And we were not gassed.
We were just not gassed.
We came out of there, and we were, like, surprised that we were still alive.
So we got dressed in those new outfits.
Already, you gave up all the possessions, except for my little ring, and we were placed in a barrack.
And from there, kids would not go out to work.
They would be left in the barrack while the parents or younger people of working age would go out on a daily work.
And the work, I understand, was basically shoveling things and picking up stones.
You know, it was like labor work, that type of thing.
It was nothing exciting.
I don't know.
I don't know what they did, but a few times, they cleaned out the kids.
So fortunately, one day I got sick, and they put me in the revere, which is like a little medical center.
And I survived that one, too.
Parents came home, and they didn't find their kids anymore.
They were just not there.
Right.
The kids were not there.
Well, actually, I got sick with diphtheria.
I almost died, so that's another time when I, I barely made it.
I was dancing with, with death.
My mother had heard that they are going to remove everybody from the medical center.
They somehow gave me an injection.
I remember receiving an injection.
Or maybe I did get better, but I wasn't ready to leave the revere.
And my mother came and asked them if she could take me out, and she came and asked permission to take me to work.
And that day, they cleared out the revere-- the medical center.
No one was there anymore, plus there were no children, except for a few.
And one of the few was my girlfriend, Ruthie, who did survive the war.
Where did you meet Ruthie?
I met her-- actually, I think I met her in in Kaiserwald, which was-- which was-- they brought in Jews from, from, from Latvia.
And that's where I met her.
I met her in Kat Zet.
And you say, Kat Zet--
Kat Zet is a camp.
Yes, it's a concentration camp.
I don't know.
I don't know.
I never did any research on this, but that's what we used to call it, Kat Zet.
I called Kat Zet until I didn't know of the, the concentration camp, which was later when I arrived to Stutthof.
So I met this girl, yes, and she and I would sit and worry about dying.
And we would [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] explore all kinds of possibilities which would be the better way to die-- by the gas, or by beaten, beating, or whatever.
And she and I decided that the best way to die would be by the bullet.
And I have to read something because after the war, she survived the war.
And I did write a poem for her for her 40th anniversary, and it has-- it traces back to our childhood.
Ruth and I, we were children when we met in Kat Zet.
We each had a mother, luckier than others, no longer a father.
We lived in a barrack under one roof, and we constantly adjusted our future, with no foundation or proof.
We felt frozen with horror and no time to borrow.
Hope seemed pathetic, but our acts of function still remained static.
Daily, we crumbled inside and had no place to hide.
We were still children, and yet no longer children.
Then one day in May, with much exhilaration, came the liberation.
We began to blossom, but life still seemed awesome.
We still talked about how we almost fermented, even though times had begun to feel quite augmented.
We started to date at an early age and explored possibilities under very modest facilities.
We met a couple of great guys who suggested that we become their wives.
In the spring of 1950, only two months apart, we each had a wedding ceremony and followed my [INAUDIBLE] laws of matrimony.
A few years later, our slender figures temporarily altered, and we began to look a little more upholstered.
Being a parent was not easy.
It kept us quite busy.
Our children are achievers and turned out to be intellectual believers.
Now, our days do not go without a care.
Thank God, after 40 years of marriage, we are still a pair.
Because of many reasons, we plan to rejoice life through many seasons.
Ruthie, you always say, we go back a long way.
I am glad that we are still friends today.
Yes.
It's by me, right.
As the poem says, we used to talk about how we would die, but thank God neither one [LAUGHS] of us died.
She survived it.
We were not together till the very end.
We lost track of each other.
We didn't meet until after the war, in the United States, I think.
Probably about 11, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] yes.
Well, [SIGHS] actually, I don't remember how we separated from that particular camp because at one point, they took a whole transport away to a different camp.
Later, we went to a different camp, too, and that different camp meant again going by train.
And actually it was more than by train.
We were on a- in a boat, and we were in a boat for about two days, the bottom of the boat, going to Riga.
And--
We were full of lice, and, we-- of course, we had no hair.
But our clothing, we would sit at night and do this, you know, get rid of the lice.
They used to put some kind of a smelly thing on us or on our clothes-- I don't know what it was-- to get rid of the lice, but we still had lice.
I could never understand.
Perhaps from weakness, I don't know.
People were full of lice.
I don't think so.
I don't remember.
They would take clothes, I think, and and they would go through some kind of a process.
But it didn't matter because we didn't-- as soon as you put it back on, you were full of lice again.
So we got it from each other.
As far as toilets, it was terrible.
Every time you needed a toilet, there was maybe one or two toilets in the entire barrack, and there were always lines.
And if you were unfortunate, you had some problem with dysentery, you were very unfortunate.
It was horror.
Then you got beaten.
Oh, yes, oh, of course, a lot of people got sick and died.
The barrack was not clear of death every single day.
Every single day, people were dying.
In the morning, we had to get up, and it was called an appell, like roll calling.
And I remember, you had to, like, really look straight at the person who was counting the rows.
And maybe I, as a kid, I don't know what happened.
I kind of turned my head to the side, and suddenly I felt such a blow right here in my left side.
I remember seeing my head falling on the floor and coming back up, down and up, until it diminished, but it was-- I thought-- I thought that he knocked off my head.
I didn't faint, no, but I was not aware of where I was and what happened to me for a good few seconds, maybe more than just a few seconds.
And actually, I had suffered from that blow was something very serious because eight years ago, I had a tumor removed, called meningioma, from my brain over here.
And the doctor asked me immediately.
He said, what happened to you?
Did you ever get hit here, because this particular tumor is prevalent among construction workers who get hit, something falls on your head.
And I told him the story.
He says, well, it looks like it was there from the war, and it just took many years to grow.
I almost died from that.
But it wasn't malignant?
It was not malignant.
It was just a tumor the size of a plum, growing and pressing on my brain.
It was, like, a half an inch from my optic nerve.
And eight years ago, it was removed, and I'm very, very lucky that I'm alive and that I'm well coordinated also.
No, I mean, physically, my motor skills.
I mean, you pay a price, you know, but it's not what it was.
But it's considerably good.
Nothing, it didn't happen.
Anything didn't happen.
The few pieces I learned in the ghetto, which one of the pieces was a piece by Mendelssohn, I knew inside out, but not having a piano, I used to sit and play on the bench.
And I'd play on my mother's back and finger, just finger the notes, and I thought I knew the piece.
It turned out that after the war, I went over to a piano, I couldn't even find where to begin.
Like, the whole keyboard looked alien to me.
But--
Oh, yes, oh, it was my greatest desire, and I was actually doing pretty well.
After one year of music, I played Chopin waltzes and Mendelssohn.
I had a very nice technique.
I understood the structure of music and harmonically what goes on, the progressions of chords.
But it somehow came to a halt, the years that I had lost.
I mean, even in the ghetto, I didn't have a piano.
Whatever practice you did, the few pianos that were available, you were assigned a half an hour, perhaps, and you practiced.
But then, then there were several years, quite a few years, actually, that I didn't have a piano, and that really was a big factor in my not achieving such a level of being a concert pianist.
Yes, oh, that was my dream, yes.
I achieved it, but not, not the, the-- the skill is not there what it needs to become a pianist.
Well, now, I do, but it took much effort to do it.
I think we stayed there almost a year.
Well, as I said, they cleaned out the children from the barracks.
I was just a few that survived, and the beatings, and the hunger, and, and-- then suddenly, they decided they would take a transport out of the camp, take it to Riga, which is another camp.
And that was the boat ride that lasted two days, which we thought we would never-- first of all, we didn't know where we're going.
Again, they never told you where we were going.
I thought that they were just taking us on a big ship.
I think it was a ship, not a boat.
And--
I have no idea.
And we were on the bottom of the boat, so to speak, the basement of the boat, where they have the sand, you know, to keep the balance of the boat, the ship.
And people were very ill.
There was no food.
There was no water.
People-- I don't even remember if people died or not.
Yes, and my aunt-- my mother told me that it was Yom Kippur or Rosh Hashanah, so it must have been '44, I think, yes.
And then we were taken again on, on smaller boats-- or on trains-- and then on a train, and we arrived to Stutthof.
We stayed, yeah.
We stayed for almost a year there.
Riga?
OK, in Riga, yes, it was another concentration camp, also big, big rooms with like a barrack-type.
And again shaving the head, and the lice, and the beatings, and the whole thing.

But we were taken to every morning-- and this included even the children-- to work for a German firm called AEG, A-E-G, where they were doing field telephones, and they showed us how to connect the wires to the receptors.
And we worked there all day.
The kids as well-- yes.
My mom, too-- no, my mom worked harder work.
I think my mom worked, I think shoving coals in a big oven.
It was very hard.
She would-- she didn't think she would survive.

Well, very difficult, but if not so my mom and my aunt, I would not be alive because [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when we got to Stutthof, after Riga, again, if not for my aunt and my mother, I would not be alive today.
So--
Different places.
I think my aunt maybe worked with me, and the advantage there was that we did come in contact with Gentile.
This is Riga.
Whereas previously we didn't come in contact with any Christians.
But in this factory, we did come in contact with some Christians, and sometimes they would hand me a little piece of bread, me and another of my friend, you know.
They would hand the kids an extra few bites of bread or maybe a piece of sugar or something.
They felt pity for us, but that was-- that was a help.
It was very helpful because we were always hungry.
I mean, I only dreamed of having a loaf of bread so I can finish the whole loaf all by myself.
You know, we were very hungry and very thin.
My feeling was that I envied the existence of a dog.
I, I wanted to be a dog so I would be cared for, so I would have this little doghouse, and nobody would bother me and even feed me.
And that's what I wanted to do.
I just couldn't understand in any sense how I am responsible for such a situation and why the Germans hated the Jews so much.
I didn't understand this at all.
Yes, more or less.
And when you worked with these non-Jewish people, they weren't hateful to you in any way?
I mean some of them, because you just described some of them were helpful to you.
Right, I don't remember the display of hate within the working quarters.
I don't remember that, and the rest of the milieu was together with my own people.
How did the people, the Jewish people, act under those very trying conditions?
It was not easy.
I do remember incidents of fights, verbal as well as physical.
Everybody was for themselves.
As they say, every man for themselves, because you knew that if you don't do things for yourself, nobody else is going to do it.
Some people were as civil as we should be, but not everybody.
Also there is a distinction, I think, and this really worried me all the time-- these class distinctions, which did happen, too.
Even there, and the worst part was when the Hungarian Jews arrived, and again, there was a definite dichotomy between us and them.
And that, too, I couldn't understand.
It was an unfortunate situation because they took every Hungarian, or maybe Czechoslovakian, or Jew.
They took them on the outskirts of Riga, and they slaughtered them, just like [NON-ENGLISH].
So they didn't even stay at camp, and then we later found out what they did with them, which was just tragic.
You see, I I don't know, but in Hungary and in Czechoslovakia, maybe the conditions weren't as harsh as what you lived under.
Probably, I don't know.
Yes.
I don't remember resenting anybody, but I do remember that there was some kind of friction.
Actually, I think they resented us, now that I think of it, and it's interesting why.
I happen to have married a man from that region of Europe, and he told me later that the Jews in Hungary or Czechoslovakia did look down at Polish Jews.
I don't know why.
It's silly, but it did happen.
The tragedy was that they were all killed.
The whole transport.
That was-- that was in Riga, maybe not late 1944, maybe middle of 1944.
Yes, some time in 1944 when we were in Riga-- actually, from Riga, we were taken to Stutthof.
That was a [GERMAN].
That was the worst.
That was the worst.
Those were horrible moments.
Again, we were given numbers, not on my hand.
We were given numbers on our costumes, uniforms, and again, we were severely beaten and hunger.

They used to call us out, 4:00 in the morning, 5:00 in the morning, for the roll call, and if somebody didn't do something just right or walked away, or-- they would give them whippings, like 25, 30, until they fell unconscious.
No, this is Stutthof.
Well, that was the boat ride.
We rode that boat for days, and then we went on-- it was in a ship.
Then we rode small boats to get to a train.
Then we rode the train for the longest time.
I can't even remember how long it was.
Days, maybe, yes, and then finally we arrived to Stutthof.
And that was like hell, itself.
As I call it in my compositions, which I will play later, the tunnel of death.
That's what I called it.
Yes.
Right, there we knew.
Stutthof had-- I saw, the chimneys.
Right, we knew that there was a crematorium there.
I don't know.
Maybe I did.
I don't remember.
I was never conscious of it.
I do know that I was again ill there, and from, from that blow that I had, it somehow impaired a vessel in my left nostril.
And I would get these nosebleeds, and I had lost a lot of blood.
I would walk out with whatever.
I grabbed a [NON-ENGLISH] of paper, whatever, to stop the nosebleed.
It would stop, and then it would go again.
And there was no doctors to cauterize it, so I had lost a lot of blood.
And I was very, very weak already, and they would only-- they were like vultures.
They would only watch and try to find people like that, especially children who were weak.
They would just take them away, and, of course, we all know where.
I don't know.
Well, I do know that one morning, they called out all the children.
They said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
All children under 13, raus, gather together.
And I don't know where the rest went, but they took many, many children away.
And then they just left a handful of children.
I guess they fulfilled the quota that day, and I was one of them.
I couldn't do it.
How could you do it?
There was no hair.
There was no makeup.
You were naturel.
There was nothing to do, no.
But I do remember when they decided to take out a transport, transport from Stutthof to my last camp, which was Torun.
It was a Polish city called Bydgoszcz, and the Germans called it Torun, T-O-R-U-N.
To Torun.
I actually wasn't too long in Stutthof.
Not too long, maybe--
--six weeks or something like this, and it was there that we found out that my father was alive.
And at one point, I think, we saw him from across the fence, far away.
I have a vision of having seen him.
No, but we had heard that he is alive, and that's where he was.
And, of course, we were separated by electric wire.
You couldn't touch the wire.
You were electrocuted right away.
I didn't know.
No, I didn't see him in the plane.
By that point, we were separated.
I found out about him jumping off the train after the war when we met.
I didn't know.
No, I didn't, no.
No, we stayed in the-- no, I don't think anybody worked.
It was just an existence.
Beatings every day.
If you had to go to the toilet, they were standing there with the whips.
It was a door, let's say, the size of this room.
And can you imagine, if you had to exit-- let's say in the morning, if you had to exit on the roll call, and people were trying-- they would-- they would stand.
The SS and kapos would stand with their whips.
And whoever was inside got hit because this was their sport.
They were Poles, Ukraines.
A lot of Ukraines.
They were the worst, Lithuanians--
They were hating the Jews.
They were actually enjoying this whole scenario.
They did it with--
Oh, absolutely before, absolutely, I had heard later that these were criminals removed from the jail so they could take on this pleasant job.
[LAUGHS] And that's-- they were-- the beatings were enormous.
No reason.
No, not at all, so it was there when they decided to transport us to another camp, and they separated again the children.
And I knew this was my final journey.
Then, yes, I was separated from my mother, and I knew that this is a death camp.
I knew I am going into the crematorium, when at the last minute, the Blockalteste, the head of the block, who was Jewish, noticed me.
And maybe she liked me.
I don't know.
She coming over to me and she said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
You little one, what are you doing here?
Why don't you go with your mother?
And I said to her, you know, but these are children.
And she actually took me by the hand and took me to my mother's group where we were standing, and this was one more time when I was saved.
Yes, she absolutely saved my life.
It was one of the-- there were more situations, but this was definitely one of them.
And my aunt.
Then we were put on a transport to go to Torun.
Interesting.
I don't remember how we got there.
